text: Highlight Clip Tom Lee: Why Ethereum Could Become a $5 Trillion Network Tom Lee: Why Ethereum Could Become a $5 Trillion Network On July 7, 2026, Tom Lee stated in an interview with the New Era Finance Podcast that at its current valuation of around $300 billion, Ethereum is grossly undervalued. As traditional assets like stocks and real estate gradually become composable, monetized, and digitized, running on Ethereum and other blockchains, he believes Ethereum could easily become a $1 trillion to $5 trillion network in the next few years. Regarding how Ethereum captures value, Tom Lee compared it to land; if everything happens on Ethereum, then Ethereum is equivalent to the land of the digital world, making it the core asset that should appreciate over time. — link

text: The Reverse Information Paradox > In Arrow's "Information Paradox," the seller risks giving away knowledge in order to sell it. > AI creates the Reverse Information Paradox > In the AI age, the buyer risks giving away knowledge, just in order to use what they bought. You pay for intelligence twice, once with money, and again with knowledge. > Seller learns more about you with every interaction, while you learn little about what it learns in return. > Enterprises need a hard trust boundary around their data, evals, and learning loops. > A company should be able to use a model without giving up the knowledge that makes it unique. text: An Investor's Handbook I - AI Optics Driven by massive AI computing demands, optical connections are progressively replacing copper from long-distance links down to chip-level architectures because photons offer vastly superior bandwidth with lower energy loss and heat While copper still holds a cost and latency edge for short-range connections inside the server rack, the broader optical transition hinges on integrating mature silicon chips with specialized indium phosphide light sources and advanced packaging

text: Playing low caps on Robinhood chain The biggest noticeable difference is how much of a liquidity generation event this has been. Comparing the betas to when Ansem had leg ups and ranges is substantially different. As a trader this is a massive incentivization for you because it gives you reason and hope to be active on smaller betas. Which will lead Vlad and the team to be more forthcoming with contributing to the ecosystem as they're seeing what positive effects it's having. Unfortunately like I mentioned in a previous post, as soon as fomo came online the grift grew exponential. Now that there's more available trading infra, it's less about narratives/fundamentals and more saturation. People are so desperate after missing the initial leg up and deployers are relentless. You truly have minimal edge when you step into those style games unless onchain analytics is your bread and butter. I feel like I'm going to sound like a broken record with this but you really need to be identifying the runners and being content with slower sized trades to make your money atm. My trading style has been very focused on improving exactly this. I kept finding myself being right on previous runners and fumbling the bag because I get too greedy and would move down the risk curve, when instead I would of been significantly more profitable and lower cortisol from just holding all my size and taking very easy 2-4x's. There's no need to force decision fatigue besides the first 48 hours of new metas, once it's solidified after the first initial big leg, you're on a downslope for liquidity fragmentation. If you don't believe me look at the pair charts vs cashcat in the other leading coins like juggernaut, hoodrat, arrow, vex etc. Not saying they're permanently topped but more likely unless a new external mechanic is introduced by the RH team which gives them second wind. All we've had in terms of narratives is memes, mostly regarding the RH name or relations to Vlad, one defi/rwa project, a couple AI/gaming projects and a few smaller ponzi's. If RH wants to survive this initial speculative hype stage, they're going to need a much deeper ecosystem integration for new projects and devs. This has been where every L1 or new chain has the biggest struggle. They get over flustered with the influx of short term attention when initially launching. Not realizing how crucial the 1,3,6 month roadmaps for onboarding genuine products and grant/incubation programs to their platform is. RH has a lot to bank off with credibility, size, capital and a very large hungry user base, however these are all great additives. The underlying offerings need to be exceptional and it's why no ones challenged Sol yet. I do think the next 24-48 hours would be the perfect time for a genuine utility runner to spawn while there's so much fatigue in lower cap memes. Vex felt like more of a reputation runner having connections to Arb and co, maybe im wrong and haven't look at the tech deeply enough. Imo we're getting past the point where you can blatantly copy Sol projects and expect a massive premium due to the fresh liquidity. Don't think the RR is there yet to bid Ansem or the Sol eco while cashcat/RH mindshare is still so high. Only becomes valuable when we see true capitulation. Still holding full bag of Cashcat. Holding well over the weekend thus far, the recent leg up felt more like parity of traders coming to terms with the narrative. Doesn't feel exhausted yet as there's been minimal catalysts or comms from RH/Vlad. My opinion can change on a dime so create your own thesis. Closely watching early next week for how they position their chain.

text: Adam Back and Michael Saylor Oppose BIP 110 on Bitcoin Blockstream co-founder and Hashcash inventor Adam Back and Strategy founder Michael Saylor both opposed implementing BIP 110 on the Bitcoin network. Back said BIP 110 attempts to police other people's transactions and conflicts with Bitcoin's principles of decentralization and permissionlessness, warning that pushing it forward would only lead to a fork. Saylor said BIP 110 turns a spam dispute into a consensus change that would invalidate some currently valid, fee-paying transactions. He argued that such a precedent is highly dangerous and that the community should focus on more fundamental threats. — link
author: admin#1534592031
